items.q: What is the time complexity of binary search?
items.misconception: O(n) - because it searches through items.
items.why: Students conflate 'fast' with O(1). Binary search halves the search space each step, giving logarithmic time - much better than linear, but not constant.

items.q: What is abstraction in computer science?
items.correct: Removing unnecessary detail to focus on essential features of a problem.
items.misconception: Making something abstract art or vague.
items.random_wrong: Writing code that does not work.
items.why: Students borrow the everyday meaning of 'vague'. In CS, abstraction is a precise technique: ignoring details that don't matter for the current task, while keeping what does.

items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: Average cost per operation over a long sequence is:
items.why: Correct answer: Amortised. “Worst case” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A tight asymptotic bound is denoted:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Big Theta. “Big O” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many standard 'cases' (worst, best, average) are usually analysed?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
